---
title: End-to-end marine modelling reveals the importance of divergent future upwelling projections for the Southern Benguela Upwelling ecosystem.

author:
  - name: Benjamin T. Grier
    email: grierben777@gmail.com
    affiliations:
        - id: UStrath
          name: University of Strathclyde
          address: Glasgow
          country: United Kingdom 
    attributes:
        corresponding: true
  - name: Michael R. Heath
    affiliations:
        - id: UStrath
          name: University of Strathclyde
          address: Glasgow
          country: United Kingdom 
#   - name: Kelly Ortega-Cisneros
#     affiliations:
#         - id: UCT
#           name: University of Cape Town
#           address: Cape Town
#           country: South Africa 
  - name: Yuri Artioli
    affiliations:
        - id: PML
          name: Plymouth Marine Laboratory
          address: Plymouth
          country: United Kingdom
  - name: Lee de Mora
    affiliations:
        - id: PML
          name: Plymouth Marine Laboratory
          address: Plymouth
          country: United Kingdom
  - name: Matthew Hatton
    affiliations:
        - id: UStrath
          name: University of Strathclyde
          address: Glasgow
          country: United Kingdom 
#   - name: Lynne Shannon
#     affiliations:
#         - id: UCT
#           name: University of Cape Town
#           address: Cape Town
#           country: South Africa
  - name: Jack H. Laverick
    affiliations:
        - id: UStrath
          name: University of Strathclyde
          address: Glasgow
          country: United Kingdom 
  - name: Douglas C. Speirs
    affiliations:
        - id: UStrath
          name: University of Strathclyde
          address: Glasgow
          country: United Kingdom 

abstract: |
    Climate change is projected to alter marine ecosystem structure and function 
    globally. Modelling these changes is challenging, partly because projections 
    of the future physical environment are uncertain, especially in highly 
    productive, and variable, ocean upwelling areas such as the Southern Benguela 
    Upwelling System (SBUS) off southern Africa. Here, we describe an implementation 
    of the StrathE2E end-to-end ecosystem model for the SBUS. The model was forced 
    by a four-member ensemble of NEMO-ERSEM physical-biogeochemical model outputs. 
    The ensemble covers two different NEMO-ERSEM forcing models (CNRM-CM6-1-HR & 
    GFDL-ESM4) which provided driving data sets (2010 – 2069) for NEMO, each under
    two Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSP) and Relative Concentration Pathway (RCP)
    emissions scenarios (1-2.6: Sustainability – Taking the Green Road, and 3-7.0:
    Regional Rivalry – A Rocky Road).
    
    We find net primary production and low trophic level guilds, such as zooplankton, 
    exhibit diverging trajectories across the two NEMO-ERSEM forcing models, implying
    major differences in the emerging trophic efficiency of the system. High 
    trophic level guilds such as birds, pinnipeds, and cetaceans are highly 
    sensitive to the parameterisation of demersal fish, suggesting improvements 
    in our understanding of demersal fish in the region are important for 
    understanding and modelling the impacts of climate change on high trophic 
    level guilds. Environmental driver sensitivity analyses reveal vertical mixing
    is highly important for variation in low trophic level guilds across these 
    forcing models. These findings highlight the sensitivity of the SBUS ecosystem
    to variation in wind-driven upwelling and downwelling, and especially the 
    need for advancements in monitoring and modelling of upwelling changes for 
    the region.

keywords:
    - StrathE2E2

filters:
    - _extensions/authors-block

date: last-modified

format:
  elsevier-docx:
    journal:
      name: Ecological Modelling
      formatting: preprint
      cite-style: authoryear

bibliography: references.bib
csl: oxford-university-press-scimed-numeric.csl
engine: julia

crossref:
  custom:
  - kind: float
    reference-prefix: Figure S
    key: sfig
  - kind: float
    reference-prefix: Table S
    key: stbl

editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 92
    canonical: true
---

# Introduction

Temperate marine ecosystems globally are facing increasing environmental change in ocean
temperature, nutrient availability and stratification [@fox-kemper2021]. Species survival
and ecosystem functioning in marine systems are vulnerable to increasingly variable
environmental conditions beyond those historically experienced, influencing the provision of
ecosystem services such as fisheries [@cooley2023]. Upwelling-reliant marine ecosystems face
particular uncertainty and low-confidence around current predictions of future climate
change impacts [@fox-kemper2021].

The Southern Benguela Upwelling System (SBUS) is a large Eastern Boundary Upwelling System
(EBUS) that extends around the western coast of South Africa, from the warm Agulhas current
in the south to the northern Benguela system (Namibia) in the north. The SBUS is a highly
productive wind-driven upwelling system, supporting large-scale-commercial, small-scale and
recreational fisheries for South Africa [@hutchings2009; @sumaila2016]. Net primary and
fisheries productivity exhibit high seasonal and decadal variability due to changes in
environmental conditions such as temperature and upwelling [@lamont2014].

Adoption of ecosystem approaches to fisheries management and Integrated Ecosystem
Assessments in the last 25 years highlights the importance of ecosystem-scale modelling for
management in the Southern Benguela region [@bornman2025; @shannon2004; @petersen2015].
Ecosystem modelling has suggested high sensitivity to plankton growth rates
[@ortega-cisneros2017], and high importance of forage fish and top-predators for trophic
control of the food web [@shannon2000; @travers-trolet2014]. End-to-end modelling using
Atlantis found that the ecosystem is likely to face predatory fish declines under warming
scenarios [@ortega-cisneros2018]. While substantial climate change modelling has been
performed for this region [@eddy2025; @lockerbie2019; @ortega-cisneros2018; @shannon2020;
@travers-trolet2014], greater understanding of the influence of uncertainty and possible
complex environmental interactions on climate change impacts and mechanisms is needed to
inform further research and management.

This investigation uses the StrathE2E end-to-end ecosystem model to uncover possible future
climate change impacts and causal drivers of change. In EBUS, such as the Southern Benguela,
conditions under climate change are highly uncertain due to a limited understanding of how,
and by what magnitude, wind-driven upwelling processes may change [@bograd2023;
@fox-kemper2021]. High uncertainty in nutrient upwelling leads to low confidence in
ecosystem response predictions [@fox-kemper2021]. Scenario uncertainty due to uncertain
future conditions plays a large role in projecting ecosystem models under climate change
[@payne2016].

This investigation aims to assess the potential impacts of climate change on the state of
the Southern Benguela Upwelling ecosystem using StrathE2E end-to-end models. This
investigation aims to identify causal relationships between environmental drivers, including
inorganic nutrients and upwelling, and ecosystem state for the Southern Benguela Upwelling
System using a sensitivity analysis approach across three decades and four possible future
climate change trajectories. This investigation highlights the importance of projected
upwelling changes on the ecosystem, particularly for low trophic level guilds and suggests
that improved understanding and projections of future hydrodynamics are necessary to
understand future climate change impacts on the ecosystem.

# Methods

This investigation uses the StrathE2E end-to-end ecosystem model (**Box 1**). Two StrathE2E
models are parameterized for 2010-2019 conditions under CNRM-CM6-1-HR and GFDL-ESM4
NEMO-ERSEM forcings. To assess potential impacts of climate change on the Southern Benguela
Upwelling ecosystem, this investigation uses environmental forcing data extracted from
NEMO-ERSEM outputs across six decades, two Shared-Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs 1-2.6 and
3-7.0) and two NEMO-ERSEM forcing models. To identify key drivers of guild nitrogen mass
under climate change a full-factorial permutation sensitivity analysis approach is used
(@fig-method-outline; B), first across decades and second across climate futures (different
NEMO-ERSEM forcing models). Variance-based Shapley Effect values are used to quantify the
contribution of variables to guild nitrogen mass variance across permutations.

In all analyses, StrathE2E models are run for a 49 year burn-in period to ensure steady
state, extracting final year outputs at steady state. Model runs are performed using the R
programming language [@rcore2025] and StrathE2E2 package [@heath2021]. Subsequent analyses
are performed using the Julia programming language [@bezanson2017] and visualisations are
created using the Makie visualization ecosystem [@danisch2021]. Code used to perform the
analyses, and create visualisations for this investigation can be found at \*\*\\\[Insert
citation github\\\]\*\*.

![Overview of methodology used to address the two overarching investigation aims. **Panel
A** represents the first aim: *to assess potential future climate impacts for the Southern
Benguela Upwelling ecosystem*. This includes projecting ecosystem state using StrathE2E
models and 24 unique sets of driving data (31 variables; see @tbl-variable-groups) across
decades, NEMO-ERSEM forcing models from the Sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison
Project (CMIP6), and Shared Socioeconomic pathways (SSPs). **Panel B** represents the second
overarching aim: *to identify key drivers of ecosystem state change under climate pressure,
including drivers of variance across decades and drivers across projected future
trajectories*. Panel B includes a hypothetical example of a climate permutation sensitivity
analysis that could be used to explore drivers of output variance across three decades. In
this example 3 decade data sources and 4 variables are used to create a permutation set of
81 ($3^4$) total model
runs.](../data/StrathE2E_paper_method_outline.png){#fig-method-outline}

## Box 1: StrathE2E Marine Ecosystem Model Description

StrathE2E combines a dynamic model of marine ecology and a static model of fishing activity,
coded as a package for the R computing environment (Heath et al. 2021). The ecology model is
a set of coupled ordinary differential equations representing the continuous-time rates of
change in nitrogen mass of 26 non-living and living guilds of material due to flows through
a food web network. The guilds span dissolved inorganic nutrient, detritus and microbes,
through classes of plankton, benthos and fish to birds and mammals. The flows represent
predation, food assimilation, metabolism, excretion, demographic transitions (for fish and
benthos guilds; spawning and recruitment), passive advection, active migrations, and fishery
harvest. The spatial resolution is coarse, in keeping with the guild granularity of the food
web - an “inshore” and “offshore” zone, linked by advection and migration. Each zone is
further subdivided into 4 seabed habitats and, in the offshore zone, 2 water column layers.
The seabed habitats are biogeochemical compartments with discrete rates of processing
detritus mineralisation and nutrient recycling constrained by sediment grain size, and the
sensitivity of these to disturbance e.g. by fishing abrasion. External environmental drivers
are monthly resolution annual cycles of time-varying physical and chemical data
(temperatures, marine hydrodynamics and river hydrology, sea surface light, turbidity, and
boundary data on inorganic nutrient concentrations and volumetric fluxes of water). The
model outputs state variable nitrogen masses of each guild in each compartment and the flows
between them, at daily intervals.

The StrathE2E fishing model integrates externally defined properties of up to 12 fishing
métiers - groups of vessels defined by the fishing gears they use - which selectively
harvest distinctive sets of guilds in the ecology model. Properties of each metier are their
habitat-level spatial distributions of activity rate, selectivity for ecology model guilds,
discarding, offal generation and seabed abrasion rates. The outputs from the fishing model
are integrated zone and guild-level fishing mortality, discarding and offal generation
rates, and habitat-level seabed abrasion rates. These are injected as parameters into the
ecology model and assumed to remain constant over all upcoming years of simulation.

## Study Area

This investigation models the Southern Benguela ecosystem, extending from the northern South
Africa Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) border to the Agulhas Bank in the south. The Agulhas
Current is a warm-water Western Boundary current that flows southwards along the eastern
coast of Southern Africa. The Agulhas Current terminates at the western edge of the Agulhas
Bank, where the Benguela current begins, transporting upwelled cold water northwards along
the western coast. This creates a distinct divide in environmental conditions in the
currents to the east and west of the Agulhas Bank [@lutjeharms2006], while injection of warm
water into the start of the Benguela Current enhances mixing [@veitch2017]. To define the
continental shelf region included in this investigation, we exclude any areas that are
situated at more than 800m depth (@sfig-distance-depth-map). Cumulative fishing effort data
[@globalfishingwatch2025] suggests that this domain definition captures a key fishing
region, with different spatial patterns of fishing occurring in deeper waters and in the
waters of the Agulhas current (@sfig-fishing-map).

We define the inshore region of the Southern Benguela modelling domain as continental shelf
with a distance to shore of 20km and depth of less than 50m. This inshore region is defined
to separate the influence of coastal processes such as river inputs and shore-based /
nearshore fishing pressure from the offshore region. The offshore region encapsulates all
areas in the model domain beyond this boundary.

The model domain is separated into 8 sediment-derived habitat types: rock, gravel, sand and
mud sediment types for the inshore and offshore zones (@fig-habitat-map). Sediment maps were
digitised and georeferenced from Birch et al. [-@birch1987]. These sediment types represent
different levels of nutrient reflectance back into the water column after settlement. The 8
habitat types are primarily used to parameterise the distribution of fishing fleet pressure
over the model domain area, rather than explicitly represent the spatial distribution of the
functional groups' nitrogen mass over the habitats.

![Model habitat map indicating the domain region and the distinct model habitat types for
inshore and offshore regions that are used to distributed fishing
effort.](../figs/habitat_map.png){#fig-habitat-map width="14.82cm"}

## Parameterisation for Southern Benguela

To implement StrathE2E (**Box 1**) for the Southern Benguela, we collated region-specific
inputs from available data, literature and regional expert knowledge, covering the 2010-2019
period where possible (@stbl-model-inputs). This includes input data representing fishing
fleets operating in the region and their catch of the modelled guilds, biological event
timing data for key regional species for each guild and observational target data for
parameter optimisation of the ecosystem model. Due to limited data availability, demersal
and planktivorous fish guilds are parameterised using data for Cape Hakes (*Merluccius
capensis* and *Merluccius paradoxus*) and; sardine (*Sardinops sagax*), anchovy (*Engraulis
encrasicolus*) and Redeye round herring (*Etrumeus whiteheadi*) respectively.
Parameterisation of the fishing model for exploitable guilds used reconstructed catch data
from Sea Around Us as official fisheries catch data were not accessible [@white2020].
Additionally, environmental driving data for the model implementation region was extracted
from NEMO-ERSEM output [@artioli2023; @butenschön2016; @gurvan2019] (see environmental
driving data section below). StrathE2E models were calibrated individually for CNRM-CM6-1-HR
and GFDL-ESM4 to preserve differing representations of baseline (2010-2019) conditions,
resulting in a "CNRM-CM6-1-HR StrathE2E model" and a "GFDL-ESM4 StrathE2E model" for the
ecosystem. For more information on the parameterisation and calibration of StrathE2E for the
region see Supplementary File 2.

## Environmental driving data

This StrathE2E investigation uses monthly 9-year climatology driving data for physical and
chemical variables annually averaged from each decadal time period. Physical driving data
include variables such as sea temperature, light, river inflow volumes and boundary flow
rates for each water volume. Chemical driving data include nutrient concentrations at the
ocean boundary of each of the volumes and river inputs. Environmental driving data are
extracted from a set of four NEMO-ERSEM biogeochemical model simulations forced by two
models from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6 - CNRM-CM6-1-HR and
GFDL-ESM4) under two Shared-Socioeconomic-Pathways (SSPs: SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0). SSP1-2.6
is selected as a climate mitigation scenario, while SSP3-7.0 is selected as a contrasting
plausible future under increasing future emissions [@hausfather2020]. The two CMIP6 models
are chosen for their different climate sensitivity, so that for each SSP scenario different
potential impacts of climate change could be explored. GFDL-ESM4 has an equilibrium climate
sensitivity (ECS) value of 2.7-3.4°C [@dunne2020], while CNRM-CM6-1-HR has a higher ECS of
4.34°C [@jones2023], meaning CNRM-CM6-1-HR represents a greater level of atmospheric warming
for the same amount of CO~2~ increase compared to GFDL-ESM4. Driving data variables are
averaged within decades, including 2010-2019, 2020-2029, 2030-2039, 2040-2049, 2050-2059,
2060-2069, to generate monthly climatologies.

## Initial climate change analyses

Under four possible future scenarios and six decades we model steady state nitrogen mass
values, averaged across the domain area (aggregating inshore and shallow and deep offshore
zones), for each modelled guild. We perform Monte Carlo simulations using ecological
parameter values randomly sampled from uniform distributions, weighting model outputs
according to observational target data likelihood (see \*\*implementation document\*\* for
information on target data) to calculate 95% credible intervals. Sampled parameters include
all fitted parameters such as guild preference and nitrogen uptake rates, as well as fixed
parameters such as guild Q10 values an background metabolic rates. All parameters were
sampled using a coefficient of variation of 0.15.

For each NEMO-ERSEM forcing model parameterisation, using 2010-2019 environmental driving
data, 1000 parameter combinations are sampled and applied to later decades of environmental
data (a total of 24,000 model runs). To assess impacts across these climatology decades and
scenarios, we visualise final year steady state outputs as decadal time series.

To explore ecosystem state variance under climate change we perform principal component
analysis (PCA) on best-fit steady state mass values, rotating the multivariate guild mass
values along two principal components. To identify guilds that contribute strongly to across
decade and across- NEMO-ERSEM forcing model variation in principal components we calculate
the average vector between decade PC values and average vector between the forcing models in
PC space. We then quantify each guild's contribution as the angle between guild mass PCA
loading and the two variation vectors, selecting guilds that strongly correlate with across
decade and forcing model variation respectively for further analyses.

## Demersal fish sensitivity analysis

Initial results from the StrathE2E models showed high parameter uncertainty for closely
linked predatory guilds such as planktivorous fish, demersal fish, pinnipeds, birds, and
cetaceans. High uncertainty in these guilds is likely due to close trophic links with
demersal fish and the high reliance of demersal fish on recruitment for guild mass
maintenance (@tbl-dfish-biology). To investigate the influence of demersal fish larvae
parameterisation on predatory guilds, we vary physiological parameters one-at-a-time.
Demersal fish larvae density dependent mortality; nitrogen uptake half-saturation
coefficient; and maximum nitrogen uptake rate parameters control biomass growth and
recruitment into adult demersal fish. These parameters were sampled in 100 evenly spaced
steps from -25% to +25% of the base parameter value from CNRM-CM6-1-HR and GFDL-ESM4 models
respectively, resulting in 300 runs for each of the two models.

## Climate permutation analyses

To quantify the influence of environmental driving data on the state of the ecosystem under
different contexts (climate forcing models, SSPs, decades), we perform permutation analyses
to calculate the contribution of each variable group to the ecosystem outcomes. To perform
these analyses driving variables were grouped according to @tbl-variable-groups, and then
permuted in full-factorial combinations (@fig-method-outline). Annual mass results were
collated for each target guild to calculate the variance-based Shapley Effect value of each
variable for each guild [@song2016].

| Variables | Variable group | Group description | Group varies across |
|-----------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|
| sslight | Light | Surface irradiance. | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| so_temp, d_temp, si_temp | Temperature | Water volume temperatures. | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| rivervol, rivnitrate, rivammonia, rivdetritus | River outputs | River output volume and nutrient outflow concentrations into surface inshore habitat. | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| logkvert, d_so_upwelling, so_d_downwelling | Vertical mixing | Upwelling, downwelling and vertical diffusivity between surface and deep offshore water volumes. | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| domain inflow variables, so_si_flow | Boundary flows | External inflow rates of water into si, so and d water volumes. Movement of water from surface offshore to inshore volumes. | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| \*\_nitrate, \*\_ammonia, \*\_phyt, \*\_detritus | Volume boundary nutrient concentrations | Ocean boundary nutrient concentrations for each water volume (so, si and d). | Decade, CMIP6 forcing model, SSP |
| so_atmnitrate, so_atmammonia, si_atmnitrate, si_atmammonia | Atmospheric nutrient flux | Deposition rates of nitrate and ammonia into surface water volumes. | Decade, SSP |

: Environmental driving variables that are altered in climate permutation analyses, the
groups allocated to variables, and levels they vary across. All other chemistry and physics
driving variables are constant across variants of the Southern Benguela StrathE2E2 model.
Abbreviations: "si" refers to the shallow inshore water volume, "so" refers to the shallow
offshore water volume, "d" refers to the deep (offshore) water volume.
{#tbl-variable-groups}

### Shapley effects

Shapley Values quantify the contribution of a variable (traditionally a model parameter) to
variance in a target model output. Variance-based Shapley Effects are an extension of
Shapley Values, which are a fair way to calculate the contribution of each variable to a
given output [@shapley1952] (@eq-shapleyvalue).

$$
\phi_j(val) = \sum_{S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,p\}\setminus\{j\}} 
   \frac{|S|!(p-|S|-1)!}{p!}\,\big(val(S \cup \{j\}) - val(S)\big)
$$ {#eq-shapleyvalue}

Where $\phi_j$ is the Shapley value for variable $j$, $S$ is a subset of input variables not
including $j$. To extend this calculation to variance-based Shapley Effects [@song2016] on
discrete inputs (levels = model variants), we substitute $val(S)$ for @eq-varexplained,
calculating the output variance explained by inputs from a set of variables $S$. The
resulting Shapley Effect for variable $j$ represents the sum of marginal contributions of
variable $j$ to all coalitions of other input variables. By calculating the contribution of
$j$ to all possible coalitions of other variables, Shapley Effects incorporate the
interactions between variables, using a full factorial permutation set to calculate exact
Shapley Effects.

$$
Var[E[Y|X_S]]
$$ {#eq-varexplained}

### Investigating drivers of change across decades

To understand how environmental drivers contribute to future change in model outputs across
decades, we perform variable permutations across three decades (2010-2019, 2030-2039 and
2060-2069) within each available climate scenario. Three of the six available decades are
used due to computational constraints. Within each climate scenario, seven variable groups
(light, temperature, river outputs, vertical mixing, boundary flows, volume nutrient
concentrations and atmospheric nutrient flux) vary across decades, with three possible sets
of values per climate scenario (forcing model - SSP combination). We present results for
guilds that have a PCA loading angle of less than 30° to the across decade variation vector
(in addition to assessment of Net Primary Production). A threshold of 30° was chosen based
on the PCA results to focus analyses and discussion on guilds that experience large
magnitudes of change over time.

Within each climate scenario all unique combinations of variable group sets are used as
model inputs. These unique permutations of climate variables are supplied to the model as
inputs across the four available climate scenarios separately.

The total number of permutations (model runs) can be calculated as:
$3decades ^{7variables} * 4scenarios = 8,248permutations$.

We then use these permutations to assess the contribution of each variable group to the
variation in model results across the decades. Additionally, we use these permutations to
identify model runs where combinations of variable groups are changed to 2060-2069, while
other variables remain at a 2010-2019 baseline. For example, from the 8, 248 permutation
runs we can identify runs where only the temperature or light variable groups were changed
from 2010-2019 to 2060-2069 and compare outputs to the permutation in which temperature and
light were both changed to 2060-2069 conditions. These permutations are then used to assess
the influence of variable group interactions and the prevalence of non-linear inter

### Investigating drivers of change across NEMO-ERSEM forcing models

To assess how environmental drivers contribute to model outputs across the NEMO-ERSEM
forcing models used, we perform permutations of variables across these two forcing models,
within each decade and SSP analysed. Within each decade, six variable groups (light,
temperature, river outputs, vertical mixing and volume nutrient concentrations) vary across
NEMO-ERSEM forcing models, with two possible sets of values per decade (CNRM-CM6-1-HR,
GFDL-ESM4). We present results for guilds that have a PCA loading angle of less than 30° to
the across NEMO-ERSEM forcing model variation vector (in addition to assessment of Net
Primary Production), highlighting guilds that have strong divergence across climate forcing
models.

Within each decade and SSP, the full factorial cross of variable group sets is used as model
inputs. These unique permutations of climate variables are then simulated across all
available decades, with variable group values treated as discrete inputs. Additionally, to
incorporate the distinct parameterisations of the StrathE2E model for CNRM-CM6-1-HR and
GFDL-ESM4 forcing data, these permutations are completed once for each model
parameterisation.

The total number of permutations (model runs) can be calculated as:
$2NEMO-ERSEM forcing models ^{6variables} * 6decades * 2SSPs * 2parameterisations = 1,536permutations$.

# Results

## Future climate impacts

Both CNRM-CM6-1-HR and GFDL-ESM4 StrathE2E models for the Southern Benguela ecosystem
experienced high levels of parameter uncertainty for mass value predictions
(@sfig-biomass-timeseries). Despite low confidence in absolute predictions of guild mass,
guilds experience higher confidence for modelled directions and magnitudes of mass change
under increasing climate pressures (@fig-percent-biomass-timeseries). This suggests that the
mechanisms driving climate-related changes, particularly for plankton and benthos guilds,
are not sensitive to parameter changes. An experimental, rather than predictive, approach,
and quantification of global parameter uncertainty, emphasise the utility of a
computationally efficient end-to-end ecosystem model in a data-poor context [@simmonds2024].
This modelling approach allows the completion of tens of thousands of model runs, helping
identify mechansisms of change in the ecosystem across a range of possible model
parameterisations and climate data combinations.

![Final year, whole-domain percentage change in mass for each decade of forcing data under
each future scenario, relative to 2010-2019 levels. The y axis represents the change in
final year mass as a percentage of 2010-2019 mass for each guild. The x axis represents the
modelled decades. The line colours represent the two climate models, GFDL-ESM4 in blue and
CNRM-CM6-1-HR in yellow. Linestyles represent the two Shared Socio-Economic Pathways,
SSP1-2.6 with solid lines and SSP3-7.0 with dashed lines. Bold lines represent the best-fit
parameterisation outputs. Transparent bands represent the 95% credible intervals for
percentage change values obtained from Monte Carlo simulations. Percentage change in total
nitrogen mass is not sensitive to the represented changes in parameter
values.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/percent_biomass_timeseries_mc.png){#fig-percent-biomass-timeseries
width="14.82cm"}

Demersal fish, demersal fish larvae, pinnipeds and cetaceans are particularly sensitive to
changes in model parameters (@fig-percent-biomass-timeseries), experiencing broader credible
intervals than lower trophic level guilds. Guild production rates for demersal fish show
that modelled demersal fish gain greater nitrogen mass from recruitment than from
consumption, with recruitment sustaining net production at a positive level
(@tbl-dfish-biology). A local sensitivity analysis on demersal fish larvae physiological
parameters revealed that planktivorous fish, demersal fish, pinnipeds, birds and cetaceans
are highly sensitive to demersal fish larvae maximum nitrate uptake rate and nitrogen uptake
half-saturation coefficient values (@sfig-dfishlar-sensitivity).

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: asis

cap = """
Biological values and rates for demersal fish and demersal fish larvae guilds when modelled for 2010-2019 (and 2060-2069 for larvae) using GFDL-ESM4 and CNRM-CM6-1-HR StrathE2E models under SSP3-7.0. Values represent the outputs from the final model year after running to steady state. `T_uptake` represents the total uptake of nitrogen by the guild. `T_defec_excr` represents the total nitrogen output by the guild as a result of metabolism and excretion. `Recruitment` represents the total nitrogen gained through recruitment of larvae to adults. `Spawning` represents the total nitrogen gained through spawning. `Net_Prod_feeding` represents conversion rate of nitrogen ingested to mass, less nitrogen lost through excretion (T_uptake - T_defec_excr). `Net_Prod` represents the total rate of nitrogen gained including recruitment and spawning processes (Recruitment + Spawning + Net_Prod_feeding). Recruitment reliance can be seen where `Recruitment` contributes greater mass to the guild than `T_uptake`.
"""
using Markdown, Tables, MarkdownTables, CSV, DataFrames, Format
cd("../src")
dfish_rates = CSV.read("./../outputs/initial_runs/demersal_fish_rates_variants_filtered.csv", DataFrame)
for col in names(dfish_rates)[names(dfish_rates) .!= "Rate"]
    dfish_rates[!, col] = Format.format.(dfish_rates[:, col]; precision=3, positivespace=true)
end
print("""
::: {.table #tbl-dfish-biology}
$(markdown_table(dfish_rates, String))

$(cap)
:::
""")
```

Ecosystem net primary productivity is projected to substantially increase across modelled
decades under GFDL-ESM4 NEMO-ERSEM forcing model (@fig-percent-biomass-timeseries) and
decrease under CNRM-CM6-1-HR forcing model. There is little difference in net primary
productivity between SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0.

Principal Component Analysis highlights the influence of decadal changes and NEMO-ERSEM
forcing model choice on overall modelled ecosystem masses. The first two principal
components explain 72% of ecosystem mass variance. Guild contributions to the principal
components (loadings; @fig-biomass-pca, B) indicate guilds that contribute to ecosystem
divergence in best-fit model mass states between forcing models over time. Benthos
Suspension/Deposit Feeders, Omnivorous zooplankton, surface layer Phytoplankton, total
nitrogen mass and Carnivorous zooplankton align strongly with ecosystem state changes across
the forcing models (@sfig-pca-separation). Pinnipeds, Demersal fish, deep layer
Phytoplankton, Birds, Benthos Carnivorous/Scavenge Feeders and Cetaceans align strongly with
ecosystem changes across decades (@sfig-pca-separation). There is little variation in
ecosystem state across SSPs. Additionally, projecting 95% credible interval ecosystem mass
values into PC space highlights high variation in absolute mass values resulting from
parameter changes (@sfig-biomass-pca-mc).

![Panel A: PCA results for final year guild mass conditions for each decade of forcing data
under each future scenario and NEMO-ERSEM forcing model. The line colours represent the two
climate models, GFDL-ESM4 in blue and CNRM-CM6-1-HR in yellow. Linestyles represent the two
Shared Socio-Economic Pathways, SSP1-2.6 with solid lines and SSP3-7.0 with dashed lines.
Dot points represent 2010-2019 decade of forcing, and arrowheads represent the 2060-2069
decade of forcing. Panel B: PCA axis loadings for each of the guild mass state variables,
labelled with letters. Red and blue dashed lines represent the average direction of change
in PC values across NEMO-ERSEM forcing models and decades respectively. Guilds that align
strongly with a dashed line in panel B can be considered to contribute strongly to
directional variance in panel
A.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/biomass_pca.png){#fig-biomass-pca width="14.82cm"}

## Drivers of temporal ecosystem change

For guilds strongly correlated with temporal ecosystem change, Shapley Effect environmental
variable contribution values indicate that temperature, boundary flow and vertical mixing
driving variables contribute to 100% of the variance in modelled mass levels. Light, river
outputs, nutrient concentrations and atmospheric nutrient flux driving variables do not
contribute to variance in modelled guild masses (@fig-across-decade-shap). When forced by
CNRM-CM6-1-HR, under SSP3-7.0 the contribution of temperature to mass variance increases
from SSP1-2.6. Under CNRM-CM6-1-HR, temperature is a substantially stronger driver of
variation for pinnipeds, birds and cetaceans compared to deep layer phytoplankton,
carnivorous/scavenge feeding benthos and demersal fish guilds. When forced by GFDL-ESM4,
three of the six guilds are primarily driven by boundary flows and three are primarily
driven by vertical mixing, with all guilds having low importance of temperature. Under both
NEMO-ERSEM forcing models and SSPs deep layer phytoplankton has distinct Shapley Effect
values to other assessed guilds, with \>75% of variance attributed to vertical mixing.
Interactions between environmental variable groups largely influence guild mass outcomes in
a purely additive manner (variable 1 effect + variable 2 effect), suggesting variable
interactions are not influential in the model (@sfig-var-interaction-hist).

Model box boundary flows from NEMO-ERSEM CNRM-CM6-1-HR show substantially different seasonal
trends compared to GFDL-ESM4, with distinct patterns of nutrient inflows and outflows in the
inshore region (@sfig-driver-boundary-flows). GFDL-ESM4 forcing data show a greater degree
of seasonal variability in vertical diffusivity and offshore upwelling and downwelling
compared to CNRM-CM6-1-HR (@sfig-driver-vertical-mixing). In StrathE2E temperature
influences the metabolism and prey uptake rates for consumer guilds, while boundary flows
and vertical mixing influence distributions of plankton guilds across domain boxes,
affecting predator distributions and nutrient uptake. While the low influence of nutrient
concentration variables suggests that the spatial dynamics are more influential in this
model of the system (@fig-across-decade-shap).

![Shapley Effect contribution values for guilds identified as being strongly correlated with
ecosystem state change over time (@sfig-pca-separation) under each forcing model - SSP
scenario. Bars represent the contribution of each variable group to guild mass variance,
with a value of 1 representing explanation of 100% of guild variance. Variables are
represented by different colours, with a lack of colour indicating no contribution of output
variance (no contribution from light, river outputs, nutrient concentrations or atmospheric
nutrient flux variable
groups).](../figs/across_decade_permutations/important_guild_shapley.png){#fig-across-decade-shap
width="14.82cm"}

## Drivers of variance across NEMO-ERSEM forcing models

For guilds strongly correlated with ecosystem state separation across the NEMO-ERSEM forcing
models, Shapley Effect contribution values indicate high contribution from vertical mixing
(@fig-across-esm-shap). Boundary flows have the second highest contribution to across –
forcing model mass variance for almost all guilds assessed. Variable groups such as nutrient
concentrations, river outputs, light and temperature have low contributions. Variable group
contributions are similar across the best-fit CNRM-CM6-1-HR and GFDL-ESM4 parameterisations,
highlighting consistent mechanisms of change (@fig-across-esm-shap).

The choice of NEMO-ERSEM CMIP6 model forcing StrathE2E strongly influences projected
ecosystem outcomes (@fig-biomass-pca). The two forcing models project different future
windfields in the region, resulting in distinct NEMO-ERSEM projections of upwelling and
downwelling (@sfig-driver-vertical-mixing; @sfig-ssp3-winds-cmip). Low trophic level guilds
and net primary production, display diverging best-fit model trajectories under each forcing
model. Vertical mixing contributes the most (\>60%) to variance for the majority of these
guilds. While vertical mixing variables (downwelling, upwelling and vertical diffusivity)
show qualitatively similar patterns under the two models, GFDL-ESM4 projects greater
extremes and variance in vertical mixing (@sfig-driver-vertical-mixing).

![Shapley Effect contribution values for guilds identified as being strongly correlated with
differences in ecosystem state across the NEMO-ERSEM forcing models (@sfig-pca-separation),
for each StrathE2E parameterisation. Points represent the median Shapley Effect value for
each guild and variable group across the combinations of two SSPs and six decades. Error
bars represent the interquartile range of Shapley Effect values for each guild and variable
group.](../figs/across_esm_permutations/median_shapley_effects.png){#fig-across-esm-shap
width="14.82cm"}

# Discussion

Modelling the Southern Benguela ecosystem using StrathE2E suggests a strong influence of
climate change on the system (@fig-biomass-pca). High importance of climate variability and
change for the Southern Benguela ecosystem is consistent with existing observational and
modelling literature [@shannon2009; @blamey2012; @blamey2015]. Despite high levels of
parameter uncertainty under present and future conditions, future changes in temperature,
boundary flows within and in/out of the system, and vertical mixing (downwelling and
upwelling) may strongly affect ecosystem functioning. There have been contrasting
suggestions about long term upwelling and warming trends experienced in the Southern
Benguela system, accompanied by spatially complex patterns of temperature and upwelling
changes [@blamey2015; @vanderlingen2018]. Additionally, range shifts in multiple species
have been documented, likely driven by periods of intensified upwelling [@vanderlingen2018].
While offshore temperatures have increased, periods of coastal cooling have led to changes
in planktivorous fish distributions around Cape Agulhas [@roy2007; @vanderlingen2018]. Fine
scale oceanographic monitoring along with biogeochemical modelling and validation of climate
model behaviour with observed trends may help to reduce uncertainty in climate and ecosystem
model projections for the region.

This investigation identified temperature, vertical mixing and boundary flows as key
variable groups influencing change in high trophic level guilds across decades.
@ortega-cisneros2018 modelled the effects of climate change on the ecosystem using an
Atlantis end-to-end model forced by low (RCP 2.6) and high (RCP 8.5) emission futures with
differing temperature levels. Atlantis modelling projected declines for most functional
groups including top predators such as Cetaceans, seabirds and demersal fish including
sharks, with temperature being suggested as a strong driver [@ortega-cisneros2018]. This is
supported by the results of our investigation which found high importance of temperature,
particularly when forced by CNRM-CM6-1-HR with greater warming represented. On the other
hand, modelling with Atlantis [@ortega-cisneros2018] and ROMS-N~2~P~2~Z~2~D~2~-OSMOSE
[@travers-trolet2014] found that changes in vertical mixing (under RCP 8.5) did not
influence high trophic level guilds. Differing magnitudes, directions and mechanisms of
change when modelled using different ecosystem representations suggest a high influence of
structural (model) uncertainty for the Southern Benguela system.

Structural uncertainty arises due to choices made in the construction and implementation of
ecosystem models such as the resolution of components (spatial, temporal, taxonomic) and
relationships/links that are represented [@payne2016]. @eddy2025 conducted a large scale
comparison of global (9 ecosystem models) and regional (4, including Atlantis and Ecopath
with Ecosim - EwE) projections of biomass under climate change, including for the Southern
Benguela region. This comparison revealed high divergences in projections for the Southern
Benguela region, ranging between +10 and -40% change in consumer biomass from 1990-99 to
2069 depending on the ecosystem model used [@eddy2025]. An in-depth comparison of modelling
for the Southern Benguela ecosystem using an ensemble approach including, among other
models, Atlantis, EwE and StrathE2E would improve understanding about the possible magnitude
and drivers of climate change impacts, aiding in informing monitoring and decision making
processes [@hill2007].

This investigation identified vertical mixing as the primary driver of variance in low
trophic level guilds across protential climate change trajectories. @cury2004 suggest that
observed ecosystem change in the Southern Benguela, particularly in plankton guilds is
correlated with changes in upwelling, and upwelling associated coastal temperatures. It has
been suggested that the Southern Benguela upwelling system experiences wasp-waist control,
where environmental conditions such as upwelling strongly influence the ecosystem via
plankton guilds, while fisheries influence the ecosystem via predation control [@cury2004].
Bottom-up control is supported by the results of this investigation, and improvements in
model parameterisation and conducting of fisheries experiments may reveal accompanying
strong top-down control.

The Southern Benguela ecosystem is known to experience high variability across spatial and
temporal scales due to the high influence of upwelling [@lamont2014; @shannon2020].
Additionally, there is little consensus on how this ecosystem, and other EBUS globally, may
change in the future due to high levels of uncertainty around future upwelling changes
[@bograd2023; @garcía-reyes2015; @fox-kemper2021]. In a global comparison of EBUS NPP
projections (including the Southern Benguela) using 13 CMIP6 models @cioffi2025 found that
differences in projected changes of upwelling-favourable winds resulted in substantial
variation in both direction and magnitude of NPP projections. These conclusions are
supported by the results of this investigation. GFDL-ESM4 projects greater monthly variation
in upwelling favourable wind direction and speed compared to CNRM-CM6-1-HR
(@sfig-ssp3-winds-cmip). Distinct projected wind fields between the two models leads to
different NEMO-ERSEM projections of vertical mixing, which generates diverging trajectories
for the low trophic level guilds in the StrathE2E models. A formal model
assessment/validation of NEMO-ERSEM's representation of upwelling, along with validation of
wind projections obtained from climate models would improve confidence in subsequent
ecosystem model applications in EBUS regions.

In the face of high uncertainty and diverging possible trajectories for the ecosystem,
climate change impact investigations would benefit from comparisons of a broad range of
ecosystem models, model parameterisations, and environmental forcing scenarios [@eddy2025;
@maury2025]. Approaches such as Decision Making Under Deep Uncertainty involve exploring a
wide range of possible ecosystem futures, helping to identify trajectories of environmental
conditions, ecosystem responses or management decisions that may lead to critical outcomes
[@kalra2014]. Exploring a broader range of futures (forcing models and SSPs) could help to
identify critical patterns of upwelling that may lead to poor ecosystem states, allowing the
focusing of monitoring and trend assessment efforts.

The results of this investigation highlight ecosystem components and drivers that should be
prioritised for further investigation. Greater parameter uncertainty than future scenario
uncertainty, for high trophic level guilds in particular, suggests data collection and model
selection are important areas of focus. Additionally, improved understanding and projection
of wind-driven upwelling trends under climate change will be important for projecting
ecosystem state into the future, particularly for low trophic level guilds. Fields such as
near-term ecological forecasting may offer a pathway to iteratively incorporate such
advancements in ecological and climate change understanding in the region at timescales
relevant to management decisions [@dietze2024].

## Limitations

High parameter uncertainty in the presented models may be due to biased or inconsistent
target data used for fitting [@hilborn1992]. Additionally, identifying best-fit parameter
values increases in difficulty as model complexity increases [@preston2025].
@ortega-cisneros2018 provided error estimates across subsets of Atlantis parameters
controlling plankton guilds' growth rates, however computational limitations prevented a
quantification of global parameter uncertainty. This investigation represents the first
end-to-end marine application of best practice recommendations for quantifying parameter
uncertainty [@simmonds2022; @simmonds2024]. While model parameter uncertainty decreases
forecast precision, it has been suggested that this does not necessarily prevent decision
support but requires the use of alternative decision support methods such as management
outcome distribution evaluation [@ascough2008; @reichert2005].

While parameterised using data spanning 2010-2019 where possible, target data for guild
production rates and P:B ratios were sourced from ecological models of the 1970-2015 period
that used departmental survey data to model fisheries [@shannon2020]. As official fisheries
data were not made available, this investigation used reconstructed fisheries data for the
2010-2019 period from Sea Around Us [@zeller2017] with domestic fleet uncertainty scores
ranging from "low" ($+/-$ 30%) to "very low" ($+/-$ 50%) (@sfig-sau-uncertainty).
Reconstructed catch timeseries for Sardines, Anchovies, Herring and Cape Hakes closely match
those reported in @statuso2023. For more information on the model parameterisation process
see Supplementary File 2. Improved completeness and quality of model parameterisation and
calibration data may help to reduce parameter uncertainty and improve the representation of
species in the model.

Interpretation of demersal fish and top-predator guilds using these StrathE2E
implementations must consider that demersal fish biological event timing parameters reflect
available literature for Cape Hake species (*Merluccius capensis* and *Merluccius
paradoxus*) only. It has been suggested that Cape Hake in the northern Benguela region
(Namibia) experience high annual variability in stock biomass [@macpherson1991] driven by
variable recruitment [@kainge2013]. This likely reflects the sensitive parameterisation of
demersal fish in the Southern Benguela models presented.

## Future work

While improved model calibration data can increase model forecast precision, validation of
the models with independent data, particularly for responses to climate change, can improve
decision support confidence [@lubianabotelho2025]. @ortega-cisneros2017 performed hindcast
skill assessment of a regional Atlantis model, finding variable performance across modelled
species, however data independent of model calibration was not available. Subject to data
availability, validation of the best-fit southern Benguela StrathE2E models presented in
this investigation using independent observational data would improve confidence in the
presented conclusions and ecosystem projections.

This investigation utilises best-fit models for climate change driver analyses. Future
investigations using parameter ensemble approaches could investigate whether model
parameterisations influence the mechanisms of climate change on the ecosystem identified in
climate sensitivity analyses, rather than using a single "best fit" parameterisation
[@geary2020].

Observational studies have suggested that trends in fishing in the region may interact with
climate change driven environmental trends in the region, altering the hazards experienced
by species [@jarre2015; @ramírez2022]. Using StrathE2E as a computationally efficient model
to explore the interacting effects of climate and fishing changes may improve knowledge
available for management decisions. Using a protocol such as Ocean System Pathways
(developed by FishMIP - the Fisheries and Marine Ecosystems Model Intercomparison Project),
which outlines a standardised approach for modelling the influence of future climate change
and fisheries trends on marine ecosystems would allow StrathE2E to contribute to valuable
model intercomparison investigations for this highly variable region [@maury2025].

## Conclusions

This investigation uses end-to-end StrathE2E models to explore possible consequences of
future climate change on the Southern Benguela Upwelling ecosystem. Climate change
sensitivity analyses reveal a strong influence of vertical mixing, particularly for low
trophic level guilds. Future scenario uncertainty (due to uncertain projections of upwelling
related winds) strongly influences future ecosystem projections, identifying a key area for
improved monitoring and modelling. While this investigation reveals that temperature,
vertical mixing and boundary flows may be important for high trophic level guilds, high
parameter uncertainty and sensitivity are present. Top predator sensitivity to demersal fish
parameterisation highlights where improvements in the representation and parameterisation of
guilds, as well as observational calibration data, would improve understanding and
projection of climate change impacts. Inter-comparisons of regional ecosystem models under
climate change, focusing on mechanisms of climate impacts will be valuable to reduce
uncertainty and understanding of future change.

# Acknowledgements

The authors would like to acknowledge and thank Lynne Shannon and Kelly Ortega-Cisneros for
their contributions to data collation and discussions during model implementation and
comments and discussion during manuscript revision. The authors would like to acknowledge
and thank Stephen Holness and Tracey Pamela Mc Gahey for their assistance with spatial
fishing intensity data. This model implementation and investigation was funded by the
Mission Atlantic project from the European Union's Horizon 2020 programme.

# CRediT Statement

**Benjamin Grier:** conceptualisation, data curation, formal analysis, investigation,
methodology, software, validation, visualisation, writing - original draft, writing - review
and editing. **Michael Heath:** conceptualisation, data curation, funding acquisition,
resources, software, supervision, validation, writing - review and editing. **Yuri
Artioli:** data curation, visualisation, writing - review and editing. **Lee de Mora:** data
curation, visualisation, writing - review and editing. **Matthew Hatton:** methodology,
software, writing - review and editing. **Jack Laverick:** conceptualisation, data curation,
funding acquisition, software, supervision, validation, writing - review and editing.
**Douglas Speirs:** conceptualisation, funding acquisition, software, supervision.

# Conflict of Interest Statement

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

# Data Availability Statement

The full reproducible investigation and links to required data sources can be found at
**repository link**.

# Funding Statement

This research was funded by the Mission Atlantic project from the European Union's Horizon
2020 programme.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supplementary Material

The following supplementary material is available at *ICESJMS* online. **Supplementary File
1:** Document containing additional figures and tables referred to in the text related to
the parameterisation of StrathE2E for the Southern Benguela system. **Supplementary File
2:** Document containing the full process followed for the implementation of the Southern
Benguela StrathE2E models including all required data sources used to build the models.
**Supplementary File 3:** Additional figures related to the results of this investigation.

## Model parameterisation and calibration

Model input data such as biological event timing and fisheries data were sourced from
regional and published sources (@stbl-model-inputs). StrathE2E biological parameters were
calibrated using regional target observation data. Calibration was performed using an
iterative simulated annealing process where ecological parameters are varied to increase the
likelihood of the ecological target data from simulated model results, then fishing harvest
ratio parameters are varied to calibrate the fishing model component. Once a suitable model
fit is reached the ecological and fishing parameters are calibrated together to the
ecological and fisheries landing target data. This fitting process was performed for a
2010-2015 CNRM SSP3-7.0 baseline model. Additional information on model fitting can be found
in Supplementary File 2.

::: {#stbl-model-inputs}
| Source | Model Inputs | Time Period |
|-----------------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Sea Around Us - fishing catch data [@zeller2017] | Catch data separated into catch power (catch/activity) and discard rates | Parameterised with 2010-2019 data |
| South Africa National Biodiversity Institute - spatial fishing pressure data [@holness2018; @skowno2019] | Spatial distribution of fishing fleet activity | 2018 - layers finalised 2025 |
| Regional expert collaboration - target data | Fitting target data | 1978-2015 |
| World Ocean Atlas 2023 - monthly nitrate concentration data | Fitting target data | 2023 |
| Literature | Fishing fleet annual effort estimates, biological event timings | Parameterised using 2010-2019 literature, or nearest available time period values. Additional details can be found in Supplementary File 2 |

: Data sources used for parameterisation of the 2010-2019 Southern Benguela StrathE2E model
and the model inputs that each data source contributed to. Full information for the
implementation process and processing and use of each data source can be found at **insert
implementation doc link**.
:::

![Barplot showing the proportion of guild landings for each guild that are allocated to Sea
Around Us uncertainty scores. Low values represent data with greater certainty. Blue areas
represent data with a "very low" score ($+/-$ 50%) and yellow areas represent data with a
"low" score ($+/-$ 30%) [@zeller2017]. "Missing" uncertainty scores are represented in green
and indicate where no score was recorded for data. Data with a "missing" uncertainty score
are largely "Assigned Tuna RFMO catch", including catch from countries other than South
Africa.](../figs/SAU_landings_uncertainty.png){#sfig-sau-uncertainty width="14.82cm"}

![Map showing an indicative distribution of commercial fishing effort along the South
African coastline (pixels) relative to the model domain area (red polygon). Fishing effort
is calculated using daily fishing effort in hours between 2012 and 2019. Pixels with effort
\> 10h over this time period are
displayed.](../figs/gfw_fishing_domain_map.png){#sfig-fishing-map width="14.82cm"}

![Map showing ocean bathymetry and distance to the shoreline along the South African
coastline (pixels) relative to the model domain area (red polygon). Pixels used in volume
specification (distances \< 20km and depths \< 800m) are displayed in the
map.](../figs/domain_separation_map.png){#sfig-distance-depth-map width="14.82cm"}

## Supplementary results

![Guild final year, whole - domain, nitrogen mass conditions for each decade of forcing data
under each future scenario. The y axis represents the mean final year nitrogen mass in
millimoles of Nitrogen per gram of wet weight for each guild type. The x axis represents the
modelled decades. The line colours represent the two climate models, GFDL-ESM4 in blue and
CNRM-CM6-1-HR in yellow. Linestyles represent the two Shared Socio-Economic Pathways,
SSP1-2.6 with solid lines and SSP3-7.0 with dashed lines. Shaded bands represent the 95%
credible intervals for output variables obtained from Monte Carlo
simulations.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/biomass_timeseries_mc.png){#sfig-biomass-timeseries
width="14.82cm"}

![Guild contributions to ecosystem separation across NEMO-ERSEM forcing models (left) and
decades (right). Contribution values are calculated as the absolute angle between guild
loadings and the respective separation vector (NEMO-ERSEM forcing model separation vector
and decade separation vector displayed in @fig-biomass-pca; Panel B). Red lines indicate the
30° angle cutoff for further
assessments.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/decade_esm_separation_quantified.png){#sfig-pca-separation
width="14.82cm"}

![PCA results (as displayed in @fig-biomass-pca) for final year mass conditions for each
decade of forcing data under each future scenario and NEMO-ERSEM forcing model. The line
colours represent the two climate models, GFDL-ESM4 in blue and CNRM-CM6-1-HR in yellow.
Linestyles represent the two Shared Socio-Economic Pathways, SSP1-2.6 with solid lines and
SSP3-7.0 with dashed lines. Dot points represent 2010-2019 decade of forcing, and arrowheads
represent the 2060-2069 decade of forcing. Polygons represent ecosystem mass 95% credible
intervals generated from Monte Carlo parameter simulations, projected into median PC
space.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/biomass_pca_mc.png){#sfig-biomass-pca-mc
width="14.82cm"}

![Distribution of residual interaction effects between environmental variables based on mass
percentage change from the 2010-2019 to 2060-2069 decades. Variable interaction effect
values around 0 mean that the interaction between variable $x$ and $y$ does not contribute
any additional effect to the output beyond the individual effects of $x + y$. The 1260
interaction values are collated across 15 'guilds' (including Net Primary Production) and 2
NEMO-ERSEM forcing models, 2 Shared Socio-Economic Pathways with 7 environmental variable
groups. Blue lines indicate the 2.5 and 97.5 percentile interaction effect
values.](../figs/across_decade_permutations/variable_interaction_histogram.png){#sfig-var-interaction-hist
width="10cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
light variable group (sslight - daily integrated sea surface irradiance). Values indicate
levels of each irradiance across 12 months of the year. Line colours represent the decade of
driving data and line styles represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared
Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/light_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-light
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
river outputs variable group (rivammonia - flow weighted ammonia concentration of river
outputs, rivnitrate - flow weighted nitrate concentration of river outputs, rivervol - daily
river outflow into the inshore zone). Values indicate levels of each driving variable across
12 months of the year. Line colours represent the decade of driving data and line styles
represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/river_outputs_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-river
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
temperature variable group (d_temp - deep layer temperature, si_temp - shallow layer inshore
temperature, so_temp - shallow layer offshore temperature). Values indicate levels of each
driving variable across 12 months of the year. Line colours represent the decade of driving
data and line styles represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared Socio-Economic
Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/temperature_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-temp
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
vertical mixing variable group (d_so_upwelling - upwelling of water from the deep layer to
the shallow offshore compartment, logkvert - Log10 vertical diffusion coefficient at the
interface between the deep and shallow offshore layers, so_d_downwelling - downwelling of
water from the shallow offshore compartment to the deep layer). Values indicate levels of
each driving variable across 12 months of the year. Line colours represent the decade of
driving data and line styles represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared
Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/vertical_mixing_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-vertical-mixing
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
boundary flows variable group (d_inflow - inflow of water from external sources into the
deep layer, si_inflow - inflow of water from external sources into the shallow layer inshore
compartment, si_outflow - outflow of water from the shallow layer inshore compartment to
external water masses, so_inflow - inflow of water from external sources into the shallow
layer offshore compartment, so_si_inflow - horizontal flow of water from the offshore to
inshore shallow layer.). Values indicate levels of each driving variable across 12 months of
the year. Line colours represent the decade of driving data and line styles represent the
four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/boundary_flows_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-boundary-flows
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
nutrient concentrations variable group (si - shallow inshore water volume, so - shallow
offshore volume, d - deep volume). Nutrient concentrations represent flow weighted
concentrations at the ocean boundaries of the respective model water volumes. Values
indicate levels of each driving variable across 12 months of the year. Line colours
represent the decade of driving data and line styles represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing
model and Shared Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/nutrient_concentrations_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-nutrient
width="14.82cm"}

![Monthly timeseries (decadal averages) of environmental driving data for variables in the
atmospheric nutrient flux variable group (si - shallow inshore water volume, so - shallow
offshore water volume). Flux values represent the atmospheric deposition rate of nitrate and
ammonia into the inshore and offshore zones. Values indicate levels of each driving variable
across 12 months of the year. Line colours represent the decade of driving data and line
styles represent the four NEMO-ERSEM forcing model and Shared Socio-Economic Pathway
combinations.](../figs/env_drivers/atmospheric_nutrient_flux_driver_timeseries.png){#sfig-driver-atmospheric_nutrient
width="14.82cm"}

![Coefficient of variation values for each driving variable across decades (left) and across
NEMO-ERSEM forcing models (right). Coefficients of variation are calculated based on monthly
values using the annual variance averaged across the remaining analysis levels (averaged
across SSPs and NEMO-ERSEM forcing models for across decade values, and averaged across
decades and SSPs for across NEMO-ERSEM forcing model values). Bars are coloured by the
variable groupings used for permutation analyses. Variables in the "atmospheric nutrient
flux" group do not vary across NEMO-ERSEM forcing models so were removed from across
NEMO-ERSEM forcing model permutation
analyses.](../figs/env_drivers/driving_variable_variation.png){#sfig-driving-variable-variation
width="14.82cm"}

![Proportional sources of nitrogen influx to guilds whose PCA loadings correlate strongly
(within 30°) with ecosystem decadal trends. Diet proportions are displayed for each model
variant (six decades, two NEMO-ERSEM forcing models and two SSPs). Rows display different
decades and columns display different NEMO-ERSEM forcing model - SSP combinations. Nitrogen
sources are represented by different colours, including benthos carnivorous/scavenge feeders
(benthc), benthos suspension/deposit feeders (benths), carnivorous zooplankton (carnzoo),
corpses, demersal fish (dfish), demersal fish larvae (dfishlar), fisheries discards, kelp,
kelp debris, migratory fish (mfish), direct intake from outside of the model domain
(ocean_horizontal), omnivorous zooplankton (omnivzoo), planktivorous fish (pfsih),
planktivorous fish larvae (pfishlar), pinnipeds (seal), water column ammonia (wcammonia),
water column nitrate
(wcnitrate).](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/decade_sep_guilds_nitrogen_sources.png){#sfig-decade-sep-diet
width="14.82cm"}

![Predatory guild (including Birds, Cetaceans, Demersal fish, Demersal fish larvae and
Planktivorous fish) mass levels across ranges of demersal fish larvae physiological
parameter values. Both GFDL-ESM4 (blue) and CNRM-CM6-1-HR (yellow) parameterisations of
2010-2019 StrathE2E models are used. Parameter values are sampled from -25% to +25% of their
baseline, best-fit, values. Each response guild is represented by a column, and each
demersal fish parameter used is represented by a row. Red triangles represent the mass value
from the best-fit
model.](../figs/initial_cc_assessment/demfishlar_param_sens_unlinked.png){#sfig-dfishlar-sensitivity
width="14.82cm"}

![Decadal climatologies of daily mean wind speed (radius, $m s^{-1}$) and direction (angle)
from the two CMIP6 NEMO-ERSEM forcing models CNRM-CM6-1-HR (top row) and GFDL-ESM4 (bottom
row). The left column shows the StrathE2E baseline period (2010-2019), the middle column
shows the mid-century period (2060-2069), and the right column shows the difference in wind
bearing (0 $+/-$ 180°) between these two periods. The colour represents the day of the
climatological year from January to December. Warm colours represent austral summer and cool
colours represent austral winter. A North West wind angle represents "South Easterly" wind
fields that are favourable for upwelling in the Southern Benguela system. Projections from
SSP1-2.6 are displayed
here.](../figs/ssp126_daily_masked_smoothed_twilight_shifted_r_difference_multi.png){#sfig-ssp1-winds-cmip
width="14.82cm"}

![Decadal climatologies of daily mean wind speed (radius, $m s^{-1}$) and direction (angle)
from the two CMIP6 NEMO-ERSEM forcing models CNRM-CM6-1-HR (top row) and GFDL-ESM4 (bottom
row). The left column shows the StrathE2E baseline period (2010-2019), the middle column
shows the mid-century period (2060-2069), and the right column shows the difference in wind
bearing (0 $+/-$ 180°) between these two periods. The colour represents the day of the
climatological year from January to December. Warm colours represent austral summer and cool
colours represent austral winter. A North West wind angle represents "South Easterly" wind
fields that are favourable for upwelling in the Southern Benguela system. Projections from
SSP3-7.0 are displayed
here.](../figs/ssp370_daily_masked_smoothed_twilight_shifted_r_difference_multi.png){#sfig-ssp3-winds-cmip
width="14.82cm"}
